"text_saheeh_international": "Allah - there is no deity except Him, the Ever-Living, the Sustainer of [all] existence. Neither drowsiness overtakes Him nor sleep. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Who is it that can intercede with Him except by His permission? He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them, and they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills. His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth, and their preservation tires Him not. And He is the Most High, the Most Great.",

"arabic_text": "ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ ۚ لَا تَأْخُذُهُۥ سِنَةٌ وَلَا نَوْمٌ ۚ لَّهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ مَن ذَا ٱلَّذِى يَشْفَعُ عِندَهُۥٓ إِلَّا بِإِذْنِهِۦ ۚ يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۖ وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَىْءٍ مِّنْ عِلْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّا بِمَا شَآءَ ۚ وَسِعَ كُرْسِيُّهُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ ۖ وَلَا يَـُٔودُهُۥ حِفْظُهُمَا ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ"

"explanation": "**Theological Significance and Virtues**
Ayat al-Kursi is recognized as the master and greatest ayah of the Qur'an, containing profound truths of tawhid and lofty divine attributes. Its revelation was accompanied by significant cosmic events, such as idols prostrating and shayatin fleeing, as narrated by Muhammad ibn al-Hanafiyyah. This ayah is also reported to be equivalent to a third of the Qur'an in merit, a point mentioned by Ibn 'Atiyyah.

**Divine Attributes: Al-Hayy and Al-Qayyum**
The verse commences with "Allah, there is no god but Him, the Living, the Self-Sustaining." 'Al-Hayy' (The Living) and 'Al-Qayyum' (The Self-Sustaining) are combined attributes, with 'Al-Hayy' being considered by some as Allah's Greatest Name (Ism al-A'zam). It is recounted that Prophet 'Isa (Jesus) invoked these names when performing miracles such as bringing the dead to life.

**Transcendence from Human Limitations**
Allah's absolute transcendence is further emphasized by the negation: "He is not subject to drowsiness or sleep." Al-Qurtubi clarifies that 'sinah' (drowsiness) affects the eyes, while 'nawm' (sleep) affects the heart. This statement unequivocally asserts that Allah is entirely free from any form of lapse, unawareness, or weariness, maintaining perpetual vigilance over His creation.

**Absolute Sovereignty and Intercession**
"Everything in the heavens and the earth belongs to Him" underscores Allah's sole ownership and Lordship over all existence. This refutes the polytheistic notion that idols could serve as intermediaries to draw closer to Allah. The subsequent rhetorical question, "Who can intercede with Him except by His permission?", affirms that intercession is solely by divine leave. Prophets, angels, and other honored beings may intercede, but only for those with whom Allah is pleased, as corroborated by other Quranic verses (e.g., 21:28).

**Omniscience and Limited Human Knowledge**
"He knows what is before them and what is behind them" refers to Allah's comprehensive knowledge of all sentient beings, encompassing their present and future states. Mujahid interpreted 'what is before them' as this world and 'what is behind them' as the Hereafter. This divine omniscience contrasts sharply with human limitations: "but they cannot grasp any of His knowledge save what He wills." This signifies that human knowledge is entirely dependent on what Allah chooses to reveal or permit.

**The Kursi and Divine Preservation**
The phrase "His Footstool encompasses the heavens and the earth" has been a subject of scholarly debate. Ibn 'Abbas interpreted the Kursi as Allah's knowledge. Other interpretations suggest it represents Allah's power or a physical creation connected to the 'Arsh (Throne). Al-Qurtubi notes the atheistic denial of such physical entities versus the belief of the people of truth in their conceivable existence due to Allah's vast power. The subsequent declaration, "their preservation does not tire Him," highlights the effortless nature of Allah's continuous maintenance of the immense cosmos, demonstrating His infinite power and majesty.

**The Most High, The Magnificent**
The verse concludes with "He is the Most High, the Magnificent." 'Al-'Ali' (The Most High) denotes Allah's supremacy in power and position, not a spatial elevation, as Allah is transcendent beyond physical location. 'Al-'Azim' (The Magnificent) describes His immense power, importance, and nobility, rather than physical size. Al-Qurtubi critiques the anthropomorphic view that 'Al-'Ali' implies physical height above creation, emphasizing Allah's absolute incomparability."

"explanation": "**The Preeminence of Ayat al-Kursi**
Ayat al-Kursi is widely regarded as the greatest ayah in the Qur'an, a status affirmed by the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ himself. Ubayy bin Ka'b narrated that the Prophet ﷺ confirmed its unparalleled significance, stating that it possesses a tongue and two lips with which it praises Allah next to the leg of the Throne. Its protective qualities are also well-documented, with narrations from Abu Hurayrah and Abu Ayub detailing how its recitation can ward off Shaytan, even when taught by Shaytan himself, who "spoke the truth, although he is a liar."

**Allah's Greatest Name (Ism al-A'zam)**
Several narrations indicate that Allah's Greatest Name, which guarantees the acceptance of supplications, is contained within Ayat al-Kursi. Asma' bint Yazid bin As-Sakan reported hearing the Messenger of Allah ﷺ state this regarding both 2:255 and 3:1-2. Ibn Marduwyah further recorded from Abu Umamah that this Greatest Name is found in Surah Al-Baqarah, Al 'Imran, and Ta-Ha, underscoring the profound spiritual potency of this verse.

**Attributes of Divine Uniqueness and Self-Sufficiency**
The verse begins with "Allah! None has the right to be worshipped but He," establishing Allah's absolute oneness and exclusive right to worship. This is followed by "the Ever Living, the One Who sustains and protects all that exists (Al-Hayy al-Qayyum)." Ibn Kathir explains that 'Al-Hayy' signifies Allah's eternal life, free from death, while 'Al-Qayyum' denotes His self-sufficiency and His role as the sustainer of all creation, upon whom all depend. This is further elucidated by Allah's statement in 30:25, "And among His signs is that the heaven and the earth stand by His command."

**Transcendence from Imperfection**
"Neither slumber overtakes Him nor sleep" highlights Allah's perfect and continuous awareness, free from any human-like deficiencies. 'Sinah' (slumber) implies a state of unawareness, while 'nawm' (sleep) is a deeper state. The negation of both emphasizes that no shortcoming, unawareness, or ignorance can ever affect Allah. He maintains perfect watch over everything, and nothing escapes His knowledge, as affirmed in the Hadith of Abu Musa, "Allah does not sleep, and it does not befit His majesty that He sleeps."

**Absolute Dominion and Restricted Intercession**
"To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth" asserts Allah's absolute ownership and dominion over all creation, signifying that all beings are His servants, subject to His power. This leads to the rhetorical question, "Who is he that can intercede with Him except with His permission?", which underscores Allah's immense greatness and authority. Intercession is not an inherent right but a privilege granted by divine will, as seen in 53:26 and 21:28. The Hadith of intercession further illustrates this, where the Prophet ﷺ will prostrate under the Throne and only intercede after Allah grants him permission and sets a limit.

**Omniscient Knowledge and Divine Will**
"He knows what happens to them (His creatures) in this world, and what will happen to them in the Hereafter" refers to Allah's comprehensive knowledge of all creation's past, present, and future. This is paralleled by 19:64, where angels declare Allah's knowledge of what is before and behind them. Conversely, "And they will never compass anything of His Knowledge except that which He wills" emphasizes that human or created knowledge is limited to what Allah permits. No one can acquire knowledge of Allah's essence or attributes except through His conveyance.

**The Kursi and Effortless Preservation**
Regarding "His Kursi extends over the heavens and the earth," Ibn 'Abbas interpreted the Kursi as the footstool, distinct from the 'Arsh (Throne), emphasizing its vastness. Ad-Dahhak, quoting Ibn 'Abbas, described the seven heavens and earths as minuscule compared to the Kursi, like a ring in a desert. This immense scale is then contrasted with Allah's effortless preservation: "And He feels no fatigue in guarding and preserving them." This signifies that maintaining and protecting the vast cosmos is an easy matter for Him, requiring no exertion or weariness, demonstrating His infinite power and self-sufficiency.

**The Most High, The Most Great**
The verse concludes with "And He is the Most High, the Most Great." 'Al-'Ali' (The Most High) and 'Al-'Azim' (The Most Great) are attributes signifying Allah's supreme power, majesty, and transcendence. Ibn Kathir stresses the methodology of the Salaf (righteous predecessors) in affirming these attributes as they are revealed, without anthropomorphic interpretation (tashbih) or allegorical distortion (ta'wil), accepting their apparent meanings while affirming Allah's absolute incomparability."
"verse": "3:1-2 (Alif-Lam-Mim. Allah! None has the right to be worshipped but He, the Ever Living, the One Who sustains and protects all that exists)",
"relevance": "This verse reiterates the core attributes of Allah (Al-Hayy, Al-Qayyum) and is mentioned by Ibn Kathir as containing Allah's Greatest Name, similar to 2:255."
"verse": "30:25 (And among His signs is that the heaven and the earth stand by His command.)",
"relevance": "Ibn Kathir uses this verse to illustrate Allah's continuous sustenance and control over creation, reinforcing the meaning of 'Al-Qayyum' in 2:255."
"verse": "19:93-95 (There is none in the heavens and the earth but comes unto the Most Gracious (Allah) as a servant...)",
"relevance": "This verse supports the statement in 2:255 that 'To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth,' emphasizing universal servitude to Allah."
"verse": "53:26 (And there are many angels in the heavens, whose intercession will avail nothing except after Allah has given leave for whom He wills and is pleased with.)",
"relevance": "This verse directly corroborates the principle in 2:255 that no intercession is possible except by Allah's permission, even for angels."
"verse": "21:28 (They cannot intercede except for him with whom He is pleased.)",
"relevance": "Similar to 53:26, this verse further clarifies the conditions for intercession, emphasizing divine pleasure as a prerequisite, aligning with 2:255."

"verse": "19:64 (And we (angels) descend not except by the command of your Lord (O Muhammad). To Him belongs what is before us and what is behind us, and what is between those two; and your Lord is never forgetful.)",
"relevance": "This verse reinforces Allah's comprehensive knowledge of past, present, and future, echoing 'He knows what is before them and what will be after them' in 2:255."
"verse": "20:110 (But they will never compass anything of His knowledge.)",

"relevance": "This verse directly supports the statement in 2:255 that 'they encompass not a thing of His knowledge except for what He wills,' highlighting the limitation of created beings' knowledge."
"relevance": "This verse contains attributes similar to the concluding phrases of 2:255 ('He is the Most High, the Most Great'), emphasizing Allah's supreme majesty and transcendence."

"point": "Cultivating a profound sense of Tawhid (Oneness of Allah) by internalizing the absolute uniqueness and self-sufficiency of Allah, recognizing that no other entity shares His divine attributes or deserves worship."

"point": "Fostering complete reliance on Allah (Tawakkul) by acknowledging His perpetual vigilance and control over all existence, understanding that He never sleeps or tires in managing the universe."
"point": "Seeking Allah's permission and pleasure in all matters, particularly when invoking intercession, as no one can intercede without His explicit consent and for those He approves."

"point": "Developing humility and intellectual modesty by recognizing the inherent limitations of human knowledge, understanding that our comprehension is but a fraction of Allah's infinite knowledge, granted only by His will."

"point": "Utilizing Ayat al-Kursi for spiritual protection and peace of mind, as its recitation is a confirmed Sunnah for safeguarding against Shaytan and seeking Allah's guardianship, especially before sleep."

"summary": "Ayat al-Kursi (2:255) is a foundational verse articulating Allah's absolute oneness, eternal life, and self-sustaining nature, free from any human limitations like slumber or fatigue. It establishes His sole dominion over all creation, His comprehensive knowledge of all things, and the principle that intercession is only by His permission. The verse concludes by affirming His boundless power and majesty, effortlessly preserving the heavens and the earth."
